title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Type 1 Prices Experience Moderate Drop Along with Volumes as Market Seeks Clarity on Presidential Executive Order
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(07th April â€“ 13th April)
  • A total ofÂ 359,328 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 15.8% week-over-week (WoW),Â while all non-zero trades also witnessed a fall of 18.60% WoW as 2,510,952 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $58.02/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 1.94%Â fallÂ WoW; All Non-Zero transfers also fell by 1.93% in price, reaching $65.58/MT.
  • The average LCFS spotÂ credit price increased by 2.17% WoW; on 15th April, the spot price stood at $58.38/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $62.63/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices did not witness any movement WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 6.67% WoW each with D6 RIN prices rising by 5.49% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

On 4th April, 2025, CARB released its Third 15-Day Package of LCFS amendments, addressing clarity and consistency issues flagged by the Office of Administrative Law. The update triggered a 9.73% surge in spot pricesâ€”from $56.50/MT to $62.00/MTâ€”driven by expectations that the amended CI benchmarks may still apply from Q1 2025 if the regulation is finalized by 30th June. Key changes include expanded support for hydrogen infrastructure, eligibility of CCS-based hydrogen under renewable targets, and reallocation of base credits exclusively to EDUs. The amendments also introduce standardized LUC calculations and tighter controls on data reporting. Also, in the RFS market, Renewable diesel production in the U.S. saw a sharp decline in Q1 2025, as producers grappled with compressed RIN margins and ongoing ambiguity around the 45Z clean fuel tax credit. The sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market also faced volatilityâ€”early gains in supply were later reversed. On April 8th, President Trump issued an Executive Order directing the Attorney General to identify and halt state laws that may restrict domestic energy developmentâ€”particularly those tied to climate change, carbon pricing, and ESG policies. cCarbon hosted a webinar exploring the potential impact of this EO and broader macroeconomic shifts on North American carbon and clean fuel programs, offering a framework for mid-term risk assessment.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_595400" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.94% WoW fall, decreasing from $59.17 to $58.02 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 1.93% WoW, from $66.87 to $65.58. Type 1 trades saw 359,328 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 15.80% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 18.60% to reach 2,510,952 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_595401" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 2.17% WoW, reaching $58.95/MT. On 15th April, the average spot price was $58.38/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $62.63/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_595402" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 115 trades were reported in the market, with 30 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $166.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 25,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.50 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 5,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 51 credits of the Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $56.00. The largest volume transfers of 300,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for price of $56.85.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_595403" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In March, the average price of Spot Type 1 credits fell from February to $58.44 amid market uncertainty due to OALâ€™s disapproval of CARBâ€™s amendments to LCFS. Average (Type 1) spot price reached $58.61 as of 13th April. Trading volumes have already reached 5.30 MMT this month, nearly equalling the total volume recorded in March.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_595404" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 7th April â€“ 13th April, total VPT recorded a 9.13% fall WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose did not witness any movement WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 6.67% WoW each along with D6 RIN prices rising by 5.49% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Be8 is The First Company in South America Certified by CARB to Supply Biodiesel to California. Be8, a leading biodiesel producer in Brazil, has received CARB (California Air Resources Board) certification for the production route of biodiesel made from animal fat to California, in the United States. The Passo Fundo (RS) unit is the first in South America to receive authorization to export this production route issued by the California air quality regulatory agency.
  • Growth Energy Commends Senate Letter Urging EPA to Set Strong Biofuel Blending Volumes, Account for SREs. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, applauded a bipartisan group of lawmakers for a Senate letter they sent this week urging the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to set strong, multi-year renewable volume obligations (RVOs) and to reallocate any gallons lost through the granting of small refinery exemptions (SREs).
  • WM Announces New, Modernized High-Tech Recycling and Renewable Gas Facilities Across U.S. WM (NYSE: WM), North America's leading environmental solutions provider and largest recycler announced the company will celebrate the official grand openings for four recycling and renewable natural gas (RNG) projects across the U.S. The facilities are part of WM's plans to invest about $3 billion in its sustainability growth strategy from 2022-2026, expected to result in 39 new or upgraded recycling facilities and 20 new WM-owned RNG facilities.
  • Hyroad Energy, Bosch Rexroth & GenH2 Launch First Zero-Loss Liquid Hydrogen Refueling Station in Dallas Texas. Hyroad Energy, a pioneer in hydrogen-powered transportation solutions, announced at Hannover Messe, a strategic partnership with Bosch Rexroth, innovators in hydrogen filling station technology, and GenH2, the leaders in liquid hydrogen Controlled Storage technology, to develop the first-ever zero-loss liquid hydrogen refueling station in the United States. The system will be deployed at Hyroad Energy's flagship hydrogen refueling station located in Dallas, Texas, representing a major step forward in advancing hydrogen infrastructure beyond California and into new high-demand markets.
  • RFA to Treasury: 45Z Tax Credit Regulation Needs â€˜Clarity, Certainty and Stabilityâ€™. In detailed comments submitted to the U.S. Treasury, the Renewable Fuels Association (RFA) emphasized the need for greater clarity and timely implementation of regulations for the Clean Fuel Production Tax Credit, commonly known as the 45Z credit. RFA President Geoff Cooper highlighted the creditâ€™s potential to enhance energy security, support rural economies, and promote innovation in renewable fuels and agriculture, but cautioned that delays and ambiguities are stifling investment and progress. RFA urged the Treasury to refine definitions related to qualifying sales, unrelated parties, and consolidated groups; revise emissions rounding practices; formally include climate-smart agriculture; and adjust wage and apprenticeship requirements. They also stressed the importance of swiftly implementing the Provisional Emissions Rate (PER) process and removing disincentives for alcohol-to-jet sustainable aviation fuel. Additionally, RFA advocated for the adoption of the 45ZCF-GREET model over alternatives and called for the inclusion of emissions pathways for fuels derived from corn and sorghum kernel fiber.
  • Montauk Renewables, Inc. and American Environmental Landfill, Inc. Break Ground on Tulsa RNG Project. Montauk Renewables, Inc. (â€œMontaukâ€ or â€œthe Companyâ€) (NASDAQ: MNTK) announces that its subsidiary, Tulsa LFG, LLC broke ground on a Renewable Natural Gas (â€œRNGâ€) landfill gas project at the American Environmental Landfill, Inc. (â€œAELâ€) in Tulsa, Oklahoma in a ceremony.
  • Growth Energy to U.S. Treasury: Unleash American Biofuels. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, called on the U.S. Treasury and Internal Revenue Service (IRS) to swiftly finalize guidance for the implementation of the 45Z clean fuels credit. In a letter submitted in response to IRS notices 2025-10 and 2025-11, Growth Energy provided a detailed roadmap for changes that would unleash U.S. energy production and unlock investments needed to restore the rural economy.
  • Bipartisan Bicameral Lawmakers Introduce Tax Credit to Expand Renewable Natural Gas and Cut Emissions. Congressman Brian Fitzpatrick (PA-1) and Congresswoman Linda SÃ¡nchez (CA-38) have joined forces with Senators Thom Tillis (R-NC) and Mark Warner (D-VA) to introduce the Renewable Natural Gas Incentive Act of 2025 at a bicameral level. This legislation proposes a $1.00-per-gallon tax credit for renewable natural gas (RNG) used or sold as fuel for motor vehicles, motorboats, or aviation. The proposed legislation expands credit eligibility to certified blended RNG and the credit would remain in place through 2035.
  • Wisconsin Lawmakers Introduce SAF Tax Credit Legislation. â€‹Wisconsin legislators have introduced a tax credit to promote sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) production in the state. The proposed legislation offers producers a $1.50 per gallon tax credit for SAF produced in Wisconsin, taking effect in 2028. 90% of the fuel would need to come from renewable sources and crops grown in the United States.
  • Grassley, Klobuchar Lead Bipartisan Lawmakers Advocating for a Stronger Renewable Fuel Standard. Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa) and Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) are leading a bipartisan group of 16 senators in urging the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to restore integrity, stability and growth to the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) and the U.S. biofuel sector by raising Renewable Volume Obligation (RVO) levels for biomass-based diesel and advanced biofuels. â€œOver the last three years, multiple biodiesel plants have shuttered or idled due to RVOs being set significantly below what industry requested and production capacity. Biofuels are a large economic driver for rural America as farmersâ€™ crops are used for feedstocks and many production facilities are located in small communities,â€ the senators stated in their letter.
  • White House Issues Executive Order on Protecting American Energy from State Overreach. President Donald Trump issued an executive order on April 8, 2025, challenging state-level climate regulations. Citing policies in states like New York, Vermont, and California, the order criticizes actions such as retroactive fines, carbon caps, and permit delays, claiming they undermine national energy security and raise costs for Americans. The Attorney General is tasked with identifying unconstitutional or preempted laws and taking action to stop their enforcement. A report on progress is due within 60 days.
  • Oregon Democrats Release Transportation on ReInvestment Package (TRIP) 2025. Oregon Senator Chris Gorsek, D-Gresham, and Representative Susan McLain, D-Forest Grove, have unveiled the Transportation Reinvestment Package (TRIP) 2025. The initiative proposes a 20-cent increase in Oregon's gas tax, introduces a 1% tax on automobile sales to support the State Highway Fund, and mandates a new â€œroad usage chargeâ€ for electric vehicles . Once fully in effect, this package is expected to raise over $1.9 billion in new taxes and fees biennially.
  • Senators Marshall and Klobuchar Lead Bipartisan, Bicameral Legislation on Biofuel Tax Credit.S. Senators Roger Marshall, M.D. (R-Kansas) and Amy Klobuchar (D-Minnesota) reintroduced the bipartisan and bicameral Farmer First Fuel Incentives Act, which would restrict the eligibility of the 45Z Tax Credit to renewable fuels made only from domestically sourced feedstocks. U.S. Representatives Tracey Mann (R-Kansas-01) and Marcy Kaptur (D-Ohio-09) have introduced an identical bill in the House of Representatives. The CFPC that was set to go into effect on Jan. 1, 2025 would be extended to 2034 from its current sunset in 2027.
